question: What is a zero-one matrix and how is it used to represent a relation?
expectedPoints: A zero-one matrix is a matrix whose entries are only 0s and 1s.
It represents a relation R from set A to set B, where the elements of A label the rows and the elements of B label the columns.
An entry mij is 1 if (ai, bj) is in the relation R, and 0 otherwise.

question: Describe how to find the composition of two relations, R and S, using their zero-one matrices, MR and MS.
expectedPoints: The composition S ∘ R is represented by the Boolean product of their matrices, MR and MS.
The Boolean product is computed similarly to standard matrix multiplication, but using logical AND in place of multiplication and logical OR in place of addition.
The resulting matrix M(S∘R) will have a 1 in position (i, j) if there exists an element k such that (i, k) is in R and (k, j) is in S.

question: Explain how the properties of a relation (reflexive, symmetric, antisymmetric, transitive) are reflected in its zero-one matrix representation and its corresponding directed graph.
expectedPoints: Reflexive: The matrix has 1s on the main diagonal. The graph has a loop at every vertex.
Symmetric: The matrix is symmetric (M = M^T). The graph has bidirectional edges for every connection (if an edge goes from a to b, there is an edge from b to a).
Antisymmetric: For all i ≠ j, mij * mji = 0 (no symmetric pair of 1s off the main diagonal). The graph has no bidirectional edges between distinct vertices.

Transitive: The matrix must satisfy M ≤ M² under Boolean algebra (if the Boolean square M² has a 1, M must also have a 1 in that position). In the graph, if there is a path from a to b, there must be a direct edge.
